ukhab_identity.secondary_codes_notes: No standard secondary codes for g4. Adjacent rush encroachment (f2a) or bracken (secondary 14) may apply where sward is deteriorating — record on a per-field basis.

sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01
sources.specific_claim: g4 definition, hierarchy and distinction from c1b (reseeded ley) and g3c (other neutral grassland)

cross_references.nvc_communities_notes: MG6 (Lolium perenne-Cynosurus cristatus) is the primary NVC community of improved permanent pasture managed for grazing. MG7 (Lolium perenne leys and related grasslands) covers more recently modified swards and those with significant annual meadow grass (Poa annua). NVC assignment to g4 is inherently imprecise — NVC communities reflect botanical composition at point of survey; the UKHab g4 code is defined by management history and modification level rather than community type alone. Degraded g4 may approach MG9 (Holcus lanatus-Deschampsia cespitosa) in wetter sites.

sources.title: British Plant Communities Vol. 3: Grasslands and Montane Communities
sources.specific_claim: MG6 and MG7 community descriptions and typical species composition

ecology.description: Modified permanent grassland is long-term agricultural grassland that has been substantially improved through repeated application of inorganic or organic fertilisers, lime, drainage improvement, and selective grazing management, but has not been ploughed and reseeded within recent agricultural memory (distinguishing it from c1b ryegrass/clover leys). It is dominated by perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne) and white clover (Trifolium repens), often with Timothy (Phleum pratense), and typically occupies enclosed inbye land on lowland and upland-fringe farms. While productive and ecologically less diverse than unimproved or semi-natural grasslands, old permanent pasture within this category carries disproportionate ecological value: soil biology — particularly arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal networks and soil carbon accumulated over decades — is substantially richer than in reseeded leys, and this biological legacy is irreplaceable on a human management timescale. The key design principle for this toolbox: do not sacrifice old permanent pasture for yield.
condition_indicators_good: Perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne) dominant — >50% sward cover — dense, leafy, prostrate growth habit
White clover (Trifolium repens) present and vigorous — 10-20% cover, actively fixing nitrogen
Dense, uniform basal cover with no significant gaps, bare ground or surface poaching
Leaf:stem ratio high — vegetative leafy sward without excessive seed heads or dead material
Good soil structure: VESS score 1-2 (friable, aggregate-rich to 15cm); earthworm count >10 individuals/m²
No surface water ponding or compaction pans evident on spade test
No significant weed grass ingress — Yorkshire fog (Holcus lanatus), annual meadow grass (Poa annua) or creeping bent (Agrostis stolonifera) combined <15% cover
No rush (Juncus spp.) invasion beyond field margins
Free-draining or well-drained soil; grazing platform accessible most of the year without serious poaching risk
condition_indicators_poor: Perennial ryegrass <30% cover — sward dominated by weed grasses (Yorkshire fog, creeping bent, rough meadow grass)
White clover absent or <5% cover — nitrogen fixation function lost
Significant bare ground (>10%) from poaching, compaction or drought stress
Visible surface compaction: VESS score 4-5; poor earthworm count (<5 individuals/m²)
Rush (Juncus effusus or J. acutiflorus) invading from wet areas — indicator of drainage failure or undergrazing
Dock (Rumex obtusifolius), creeping thistle (Cirsium arvense) or nettle (Urtica dioica) infestations — indicator of localised nutrient enrichment or overgrazing damage
Tussocky, uneven sward structure — evidence of set stocking without rotational rest periods
Surface poaching, wheel ruts, or trafficking damage — indicator of management timing failure or excessive stocking on wet soils
ecology.years_unimproved: variable — typically 10+ years since last full cultivation and reseed; old permanent pasture within this code may be 30-100+ years; often 'unknown' in practice. Record farm-specific value where known.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Excellent
condition_grade_descriptors.description: High-performing permanent pasture at or near productive ceiling. Dense, ryegrass-dominant sward (>70% PRG + white clover), vigorous clover, no significant weed grass, free-draining and structurally sound soil. Well-managed rotationally. Forage yield approaching 7,000-9,000 kg DM/ha/yr under moderate Scottish beef/sheep system inputs.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Good
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Good productive permanent pasture with some minor weed grass ingress or clover decline. PRG + white clover 50-70% cover. Yield 5,000-7,000 kg DM/ha/yr estimated. Manageable without immediate intervention.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Moderate
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Sward significantly modified by weed grass ingress, clover failure, compaction or drainage issues. PRG + white clover 30-50% cover. Yield estimated 3,500-5,000 kg DM/ha/yr. Intervention (oversowing, drainage, soil improvement) or reseed planning appropriate.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Poor
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Substantially degraded — low productive species composition (<30% PRG + white clover), rush encroachment, significant bare ground or compaction. Yield estimated 2,000-3,500 kg DM/ha/yr. Approaching reclassification boundary with g3c or f2a depending on rush/wetland component. Consider whether reseed or habitat transition serves the farming system better.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Transitional — reclassify
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Sward composition and management history no longer consistent with g4. Reclassify to: c1b (if reseed planned or recent), g3c (if weed grass dominant but not wet), f2a (if rush/rush pasture dominant), or g1b (if acidified, upland fringe, Agrostis-Festuca community establishing).

observable_field_criteria: Walk field in W pattern — estimate % cover PRG + white clover by eye or sward stick. Target >70%.
Check clover stolon density at 5 random points — vigorous stolons >5cm visible at soil surface
Soil spade test at 2 points: friable, aggregate-rich structure to 15cm; no compaction pan
Earthworm count: collect 2 soil cores (20cm x 20cm x 20cm), count individuals — target >10/m²
No weed grasses visually obvious at normal walking pace
grade_field_indicators.source_notes: PRG 70% threshold: NatureScot Biodiversity Audit Grassland Selector Tool (>30% = improved; target >70% for Grade 1). VESS and earthworm count: NatureScot Thematic Document - Grassland (2023).

observable_field_criteria: PRG + white clover 30-50% estimated cover — Yorkshire fog or AMG visually obvious in patches
Clover sparse or absent in patches — yellowing of sward where N fixation lost
Soil structure variable — some compaction evident; earthworm count 5-10/m²
Some surface tracking, poaching, or bare ground visible (5-10% of field area)
grade_field_indicators.source_notes: Estimated field criteria — pending verification against SRUC/AHDB sward assessment guidance.

observable_field_criteria: PRG + white clover <30% — NatureScot threshold below which sward approaches unimproved classification
Rush (Juncus effusus) present beyond margins — any cover >5% indicates drainage or management failure
VESS score 4-5 at multiple spade test points; earthworm count <5/m²
Bare ground >10% field area; poaching damage evident
grade_field_indicators.source_notes: 30% threshold: NatureScot Biodiversity Audit Grassland Selector Tool (L2) and AECS Species-rich Grassland Management guidance (L2) — below 30% improved species = classed as unimproved or semi-improved.

forage_yield_literature.notes: 9,000 kg DM/ha is the QMS-cited maximum annual yield for long-term grassland in a Scottish beef and sheep system context — this represents Grade 1 ceiling under moderate input management. The 4,000 kg DM/ha lower bound is estimated for Grade 4 degraded permanent pasture with minimal inputs, consistent with the UK NEA semi-natural grassland range (2,000-8,000 kg DM/ha) and the Woodland Grazing Toolbox Agrostis-Festuca figure (3,000-6,000 kg DM/ha) as a proxy for the degraded end. All values refer to total annual above-ground herbage production. Grades 2-4 figures are estimated pending TN726 yield table extraction.

sources.title: Better Soil and Grassland Management for Scottish Beef and Lamb Producers
sources.specific_claim: Maximum annual yield for long-term grassland 9t DM/ha vs 12t DM/ha for new ley

sources.title: UK National Ecosystem Assessment Technical Report: Broad Habitats Chapter 6
sources.specific_claim: Improved grassland with fertiliser 10-12t DM/ha/yr; semi-natural hay meadow 2-8t DM/ha/yr; addition of fertilisers increases yearly DM yields to about 10-12t/ha

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Based on QMS 9t DM/ha maximum for long-term grassland (L2). Upper end approaches improved ley productivity. Assumes moderate N input and good management in a Scottish beef/sheep context. Not reseeded ley figures.
Interpolated between Grade 1 and Grade 3. Pending TN726 yield tables (manual extraction from fas.scot PDF required).

Weed grass ingress, clover failure or compaction reducing yield. Consistent with QMS N-response data: 50kg N/ha gives extra 2,000-3,000 kg DM/ha — absence of adequate inputs at this grade would suppress yield significantly. Pending sourcing.

Degraded permanent grassland approaching unimproved grassland yield range. UK NEA cites upland acid grassland at 1,500-5,000 kg DM/ha; Agrostis-Festuca at 3,000-6,000 kg DM/ha (Woodland Grazing Toolbox, L6 via L2). Used as a proxy floor for heavily degraded g4. Flag for sourcing.
Grade 5 = reclassify. Forage yield assessment no longer the primary metric. Reclassify to c1b, g3c, or f2a as appropriate.

nutritional_quality_by_grade.notes: AHDB Forage for Knowledge data for well-managed Scottish grazed grass: 12 MJ ME/kg DM leafy vegetative sward; CP 18-27% in spring. These figures are for intensively managed high-input rotational systems (c1b context) — applied here as the Grade 1 ceiling for g4. Permanent pasture nutritional quality at Grade 1 likely slightly lower due to sward species diversity (ryegrass ME ~12 MJ, Yorkshire fog ~10 MJ). Pending specific g4 nutritional data.
Estimated. Weed grass dominance (Yorkshire fog, AMG) substantially reduces ME — Germinal data cites 65% D-value midseason for 50% PRG sward vs 70% for 75% PRG. Pending dedicated sourcing.

ecological_value_grade_notes: Grade 3 (moderate) as a default for standard managed g4. Adjust to Grade 2 (high) for old permanent pasture (30+ years unimproved, confirmed by field species assessment or farm records), where soil biology and carbon stocks are substantially above those of reseeded equivalents. Grade 4 for heavily modified recent-input g4 with minimal soil biological diversity. Grade 1 is reserved for fields approaching semi-natural status (approaching g3c or g3b characteristics — reclassify). The is_permanent_pasture flag and years_unimproved field should inform ecological value grade assignment on a per-field basis.
ecological_value_notes:
Permanent pasture within the g4 category carries ecological value that its production figures do not reflect. Three mechanisms:

1. SOIL CARBON. UK NEA (2011) places neutral grassland soil carbon at 62.4t C/ha in the top 15cm — above improved grassland and arable land. NatureScot (2023) notes that 'due to lack of soil disturbance, the soils of unimproved grasslands are particularly valuable at storing carbon.' Permanent pasture — even where modified by fertiliser inputs — retains this carbon advantage over reseeded leys because cultivation and ploughing immediately and substantially disrupts soil aggregate structure and releases stored carbon.

2. MYCORRHIZAL NETWORKS. Perennial grasslands support higher densities of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal (AMF) hyphae than any other plant community. AMF networks connect plant root systems, regulate nutrient flow between plants, and influence soil structure through glomalin production. Critically, AMF community composition is primarily shaped by soil history rather than plant history (Springer Biology and Fertility of Soils, 2024, L1) — meaning that once a permanent pasture is ploughed, the accumulated soil biological legacy is disrupted and cannot be restored simply by replanting the same species. A 12-year alpine meadow restoration study found that aggregate stability had not fully recovered to undegraded levels after this period (Wiley Ecological Indicators, 2024, L1). UK-specific timescales are not yet established in the literature at L1-L3 — flag this as pending research.

3. IRREVERSIBILITY. Ploughing permanent pasture causes immediate, quantified losses: Irish peer-reviewed research recorded a 3.1t N/ha loss from topsoil and 13.4kg soluble organic N/ha in under two years following renovation (Nutrient Cycling in Agroecosystems, 2013, L1). UK research confirms post-plough and reseeding periods account for disproportionately high proportions of sediment fluxes from farm platforms (North Wyke Farm Platform, PMC 2020, L1). These losses are not recovered on production timescales.

Design principle: the toolbox records these values separately from production grade precisely because the farm design decision — whether to reseed for yield or protect for ecological capital — requires seeing both scores honestly. Do not sacrifice old permanent pasture for DM production gain.
ecosystem_functions: Forage production — moderate to good DM yield on a permanent, low-disturbance basis
Soil carbon storage — neutral grassland soils hold 62.4t C/ha in top 15cm (UK NEA 2011); above improved grassland and arable
Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal network support — higher AMF density than any other plant community in diverse perennial grassland
Nitrogen fixation via white clover (Trifolium repens) — reducing synthetic N requirement at Grade 1-2

Pollinator habitat — permanent sward provides year-round foraging continuity for bumblebees and solitary bees (TN639, SAC 2011); white clover and clover-associated forbs key pollen sources for long-tongued species
Hydrological regulation — intact permanent sward provides lower erosion rates (0.17-1.38t/ha/yr) vs arable (up to 66t/ha/yr) (North Wyke Farm Platform, L1)
Cultural heritage — old permanent pasture on upland-fringe farms frequently associated with traditional fell farming practices and field boundary systems with historical significance

target_species_composition.role: Primary production species; dominant structural grass
Secondary production grass; particularly suited to hay/silage systems
Minor constituent — indicator of moist/shaded conditions; acceptable in small amounts

transition_pathway_from.mechanism: Ley not reseeded after 5+ years — PRG persists, clover declines, other species establish. Modified permanent grassland character develops over 10-15+ years without cultivation.

Intensification — application of N/P/K fertiliser, lime, and selective grazing pressure shifts species composition towards ryegrass dominance. Semi-improved neutral grassland transitions to g4 over 5-15 years of increasing inputs.

transition_pathway_to.mechanism: Ploughing and reseeding — immediate transition. Highest yield gain but permanent loss of accumulated soil biology and carbon. Not recommended on old permanent pasture.
transition_pathway_to.ecological_cost: High — irreversible on management timescale; soil carbon and AMF network disruption quantified

transition_pathway_to.mechanism: Reduction or cessation of fertiliser inputs; light grazing; delayed cutting to allow forb establishment. Ryegrass gives ground to more diverse sward over 5-15 years. Yellow rattle (Rhinanthus minor) introduction can accelerate by suppressing competitive grasses.
transition_pathway_to.ecological_cost: Low — production loss but ecological gain; carbon stocks maintained

transition_pathway_to.mechanism: Drainage failure, undergrazing, or management abandonment — rush (Juncus effusus/acutiflorus) encroachment replaces grazed sward. Negative trajectory from g4.
transition_pathway_to.ecological_cost: Variable — rush pasture has some wader habitat value but represents production loss and management challenge

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Primary grazing animal for permanent pasture. Less selective than sheep; maintains sward structure without excessive flower head grazing. Native and hardy breeds suitable for wetter/upland-fringe g4. Avoid overwintering on poorly drained permanent pasture — poaching and compaction risk.

Suitable for g4 at appropriate stocking density. Selective grazing can maintain tight sward. Risk of overgrazing on fragile or poached soils. Avoid supplementary feeding on field — localised enrichment damages sward.

optimal_grazing_animals.animal: Native ponies — Highland, Fell, Dales, Eriskay
optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Suitable for lightly managed g4 in upland fringe contexts. Less selective than other ponies; tolerant of coarser permanent sward. Avoid horse grazing on species-rich grassland verging on g3c — latrine behaviour damages sward (TN629, SAC 2010).

unsuitable_animals.animal: Pigs (Iron Age, Tamworth, GOS, all breeds)
unsuitable_animals.reason: Rootling and wallowing behaviour destroys sward structure and soil. Appropriate for woodland or sacrificed areas only — not permanent pasture.

timing_windows.spring_turnout: When sward height reaches 6-8cm (approximately 1,500-1,800 kg DM/ha cover); avoid turning out on waterlogged soils before structural recovery
timing_windows.summer_grazing: Year-round rotational grazing or set stocking depending on system; avoid extended periods of overgrazing that expose soil in summer drought conditions
timing_windows.autumn_rest: Allow sward to close at 8-10cm cover before winter; avoid carrying too high a cover into winter (disease risk, poaching from heavy sward)
timing_windows.winter_management: Avoid overwintering cattle on poorly drained permanent pasture; sacrificed fields or housed systems preferable on compaction-prone soils. Permanent pasture better suited to outwintering than reseeded leys due to dense basal cover (AHDB, L5).
timing_windows.conservation_caveat: Where g4 is managed with ecological objectives (approaching g3c trajectory), see TN686 (FAS Scotland, 2024) for conservation grazing stocking rates by habitat type. Summer exclusion or very light grazing required if allowing forb establishment.

intensity_guidance: Stocking intensity for productive g4: calibrate to maintain target pre-grazing sward height of 2,800-3,000 kg DM/ha; graze to residual of 1,500 kg DM/ha / 4cm. Avoid sustained overgrazing that exposes soil or removes root reserves. For conservation-oriented g4 management (ecological value objective): see TN686 (FAS Scotland, September 2024) — full stocking rate tables not yet retrieved; flagged as outstanding. TN629 (SAC 2010) cites 0.5 LU/ha or less for summer grazing where flowering and seed set is the objective (applicable where g4 is being transitioned towards g3c).
monitoring_protocol: 1. Annual sward walk — estimate PRG + white clover % cover by field in W pattern (30+ sward height readings). Record weed grass species and estimated % cover. 2. Soil assessment every 3-5 years — VESS (Visual Evaluation of Soil Structure) to 20cm; earthworm count (2 x 20cm cubes per field); pH and NPK soil test. 3. Plate meter readings — record sward height and estimated DM cover at entry and exit of grazing events to track actual yield data over time. Log in forage_yield_actual field as farm-specific layer. 4. For fields with ecological value objective: annual species list (May-July); photograph at fixed points for trend monitoring. Note any forb establishment.

bng_unit_value.notes: BNG metric values for Modified Grassland are habitat-condition-dependent and calculated via the Defra Biodiversity Metric tool (v4.0). Modified grassland in poor condition scores lower than good condition; condition uplift opportunities exist through reduced inputs and species diversification. Confirm current BNG unit values via Defra Metric at point of application — not hardcoded here as values change between metric versions.

key_constants: Cynosurus cristatus (crested dog's tail — common in older permanent pasture)
Holcus lanatus (Yorkshire fog — constant weed grass indicator)
key_positive_indicators: Trifolium repens (white clover) — vigorous, indicating good N fixation and sward health
Cynosurus cristatus (crested dog's tail) — indicator of long-term permanent pasture with no recent cultivation
Plantago lanceolata (ribwort plantain) — indicator of diverse permanent sward; transition species towards g3c
Lolium perenne — leafy vegetative growth (not reproductive/stemmy) indicating good rotational management
Earthworm abundance — biological indicator of soil structure and organic matter cycling
key_negative_indicators: Holcus lanatus (Yorkshire fog) at high cover — poor productivity, poor N response, indicator of declining sward quality
Poa annua (annual meadow grass) — short-lived, low yield, indicator of open bare ground and sward damage
Agrostis stolonifera (creeping bent) — persistent weed grass on poorly drained or set-stocked swards
Juncus effusus / J. acutiflorus (soft/sharp-flowered rush) — drainage failure indicator; beyond field margins signals g4→f2a trajectory
Rumex obtusifolius (broad-leaved dock) — localised overgrazing, poaching or slurry damage
Cirsium arvense (creeping thistle) — overgrazing or sward damage; spreads rapidly in open sward
Bare ground >5% — poaching, compaction or drought damage

sources.title: How to improve feed quality and save money by maximising the potential of grazing grass
sources.specific_claim: Scottish dairy farms: well-managed grass yields 15.3-17.9t DM/ha/yr; individual paddocks 11.5-13.0t DM/ha; growth rates 100-120 kg DM/ha/day in June. NOTE: these figures are for high-input reseeded rotational ryegrass platforms (c1b Grade 1 context) — not g4. Filed here as comparative ceiling reference only.

fields_partially_populated: forage_yield_literature — min/max range sourced (L1/L2) but precise grade-by-grade breakdowns require TN726 yield tables (manual extraction pending)

nutritional_quality_by_grade — requires AHDB or AFBI data specific to long-term permanent pasture (not high-input ryegrass ley)

mycorrhizal network recovery timescale for UK permanent pasture — no L1-L3 UK-specific timescale identified; closest proxy 12+ years from Tibetan alpine study; flag for targeted literature search (Fitter, Merryweather, Grime — Sheffield grassland AMF work)
monitoring_protocol quantitative thresholds — requires SRUC or NatureScot sward assessment protocol with specific numeric targets per grade
bng_unit_value — confirm via Defra Biodiversity Metric v4.0 at point of application
nvc_communities — MG6/MG7 assigned but not verified against NVC survey data for this habitat category
nutritional_quality winter grass — AHDB data (11 MJ ME/kg DM average November onwards) noted but not integrated into grade table
data_completeness.notes: The ecological value case for permanent pasture is strongly sourced at L1/L2. The forage yield data for Grade 1 is sourced at L2 (QMS) and corroborated by UK NEA (L1). Grades 2-4 yield figures are estimated and must be clearly labelled as such until TN726 and/or GrassCheckGB data are retrieved. Nutritional quality data is the weakest field — available data is for high-input reseeded systems and may not accurately represent g4 permanent pasture. This record is ready for use in the toolbox with data_completeness fields displayed to users — the honest flagging of estimated fields is a feature, not a deficiency.
